requirements: The core contributions of 'SmoothQuant: Accurate and Efficient Post-Training Quantization for Large Language Models' have been reproduced, including the per-channel smoothing transformation that migrates quantization difficulty from activations to weights, the O1-O3 W8A8 quantization settings, and the accuracy, speedup, and memory results across OPT, BLOOM, GLM-130B, and other LLMs.

sub_tasks.requirements: The integer INT8 uniform quantization primitives and granularity levels (per-tensor, per-token, per-channel, group-wise; static vs dynamic) have been implemented as described in the Preliminaries section.

The INT8 uniform quantization function (Equation 1) has been implemented: X_INT8 = round(X_FP16 / Delta), where Delta = max(|X|) / (2^(N-1) - 1) with N=8, using symmetric quantization with rounding and preserving outliers via the maximum absolute value scaling.
The rounding operation in Equation 1 has been implemented as round-to-nearest-integer (the rounding function ⌈·⌋ applied to X_FP16 / Delta), producing integer-valued outputs in the INT8 range.

The quantization step size Delta has been implemented as Delta = max(|X|) / (2^(N-1) - 1) with N=8, giving Delta = max(|X|) / 127, using the maximum absolute value of the tensor to preserve outliers as described in Equation 1 and Section 2.

The quantization function assumes symmetric quantization centered at 0, mapping the range [-max(|X|), max(|X|)] to [-127, 127] for INT8 (N=8), as described in Section 2 (with the note that asymmetric cases with a zero-point offset are handled separately for cases such as after ReLU).
Per-tensor quantization has been implemented, using a single quantization step size Delta for the entire weight or activation matrix, as defined in Section 2 and Figure 3.

Per-token quantization has been implemented for activations, using a different quantization step size for each token (row of the activation matrix X of shape T x C_i), as defined in Section 2 and Figure 3.

Per-channel quantization has been implemented for weights, using a different quantization step size for each output channel of the weight matrix W (C_o dimension), as defined in Section 2 and Figure 3.

Group-wise quantization has been implemented as a coarse-grained variant of per-channel quantization, using different quantization step sizes for different channel groups within the weight matrix, as defined in Section 2 and Figure 3.

Static quantization has been implemented, where the quantization step size Delta is computed offline using calibration samples (512 random sentences from the Pile dataset) and fixed at inference time, as described in Section 2.
Dynamic quantization has been implemented, where the quantization step size Delta is computed at runtime from the actual activation statistics of each input, as described in Section 2.

The hardware-compatible post-GEMM rescaling formulation (Equation 2) has been implemented: Y = diag(Delta_X_FP16) * (X_INT8 * W_INT8) * diag(Delta_W_FP16), where scaling is applied only along the outer dimensions (token dimension T for activations, output channel dimension C_o for weights) after the INT8 matrix multiplication completes, consistent with INT8 GEMM kernel constraints described in Section 3.

The inner INT8 matrix multiplication (X_INT8 * W_INT8) has been implemented using an INT8 GEMM kernel (e.g., CUTLASS INT8 GEMM), producing an integer or higher-precision intermediate result before any rescaling is applied, consistent with the inner term of Equation 2 and the hardware kernel constraints described in Section 3.

The activation rescaling diag(Delta_X_FP16) in Equation 2 has been implemented as a per-token (outer token dimension T) FP16 scaling applied to the left of the INT8 GEMM result, after the matrix multiplication completes, not inserted mid-kernel, consistent with the constraint that scaling can only be performed along the outer dimensions of the matrix multiplication described in Section 3.

The weight rescaling diag(Delta_W_FP16) in Equation 2 has been implemented as a per-output-channel (outer C_o dimension) FP16 scaling applied to the right of the INT8 GEMM result, after the matrix multiplication completes, not inserted mid-kernel, consistent with the constraint that scaling can only be performed along the outer dimensions of the matrix multiplication described in Section 3.

The full Equation 2 composition Y = diag(Delta_X_FP16) * (X_INT8 * W_INT8) * diag(Delta_W_FP16) has been implemented with rescaling strictly after the INT8 GEMM completes (not interleaved with the GEMM), reflecting the hardware constraint that high-throughput Tensor Core MMAs do not tolerate insertion of lower-throughput conversion or FMA instructions mid-sequence, as described in Section 3.

The implementation correctly does NOT apply per-channel activation quantization scaling (i.e., scaling along the inner C_i dimension of activations) inside the GEMM kernel, reflecting the paper's statement in Section 3 that per-channel activation quantization is incompatible with INT8 GEMM kernels because scaling can only be performed along the outer dimensions T (for activations) and C_o (for weights), as captured by Equation 2.
The analysis of activation quantization difficulty has been reproduced, including the outlier visualization and the simulated per-channel activation quantization experiment in Table 1.

Code has been implemented to visualize the input activations and weights of a linear layer with large quantization error, reproducing Figure 4 (left). The visualization must show that activation outliers exist in a small fraction of fixed channels (persisting across all tokens, shown in red), that the scale of outliers is ~100x larger than most activation values, and that the weight distribution is comparatively uniform and flat.

The reproduction demonstrates that outliers persist in fixed channels: if one channel has an outlier, it persistently appears in all tokens (as visible in Figure 4, red channels), meaning the variance between token magnitudes within an outlier channel is small, while the variance across channels for a given token is large.

Code has been implemented to extract and visualize the per-channel activation magnitudes of a linear layer (as in Figure 4, left), clearly showing that outlier channels (highlighted in red) have magnitudes ~100x larger than non-outlier channels, demonstrating that activations are harder to quantize than weights.

The reproduction demonstrates that outlier channels are fixed: if a channel exhibits an outlier for one token, it persistently exhibits an outlier for all tokens across the token dimension T. This is verified by showing that the set of outlier channels (red channels in Figure 4) remains constant across all tokens in the activation matrix X of shape T x C_i.

The reproduction demonstrates that the variance of activation magnitudes across tokens within a single outlier channel is small: i.e., for a fixed outlier channel j, the values |X_{t,j}| across all tokens t are consistently large with low variance between them, as described in Section 3 and visible in Figure 4 (red columns showing consistently elevated magnitudes).

The reproduction demonstrates that the variance of activation magnitudes across channels for a given token is large: i.e., for a fixed token t, some channels j have very large |X_{t,j}| (outlier channels) while most channels have small values, resulting in high cross-channel variance per token, as described in Section 3 and visible in Figure 4 (left panel showing sparse large-magnitude channels).

The reproduction demonstrates that outliers appear in only a small fraction of the total input channels C_i, not uniformly distributed across all channels, as stated in Section 3 ('Outliers appear in a small fraction of the channels'). The visualization in Figure 4 (left) confirms this by showing that only a few columns (channels) are highlighted in red as outliers.

The reproduction verifies the effective quantization bits analysis described in Section 3: for per-tensor quantization, if the maximum magnitude of channel i is m_i and the maximum value of the whole matrix is m, the effective quantization levels of channel i is 2^8 * m_i / m. For non-outlier channels where m_i << m, this results in only 2-3 effective quantization levels, confirming why per-tensor quantization leads to large quantization errors for non-outlier channels.

The effective quantization levels formula has been implemented: for per-tensor quantization of a matrix with global maximum m, the effective quantization levels for channel i with maximum magnitude m_i is computed as 2^8 * m_i / m = 256 * m_i / m, as stated in Section 3. The implementation computes per-channel maximum magnitudes m_i and the global matrix maximum m from an activation tensor, then applies this formula to each channel.

The implementation computes the per-channel maximum magnitude m_i for each input channel of an activation tensor X of shape T x C_i, specifically computing m_i = max(|X_j|) for each channel j = 1, 2, ..., C_i by taking the maximum absolute value across the token dimension T, as described in Section 3.

The implementation computes the global matrix maximum magnitude m = max(|X|) over the entire activation tensor X (across all tokens T and all channels C_i), representing the global maximum used as the denominator in the per-tensor quantization step size Delta = m / 127 and in the effective quantization levels formula, as stated in Section 3.

The implementation applies the effective quantization levels formula 2^8 * m_i / m = 256 * m_i / m to each channel i of the activation tensor, using the per-channel maximum magnitude m_i and the global maximum m computed in the previous steps, producing a per-channel vector of effective quantization levels as described in Section 3.

The reproduction verifies that for outlier channels (where m_i is close to the global maximum m), the effective quantization levels 2^8 * m_i / m is close to 256 (the full INT8 range), confirming that outlier channels consume nearly all available quantization resolution under per-tensor quantization, as described in Section 3.

The reproduction verifies that for non-outlier channels (where m_i << m, i.e., m_i is ~100x smaller than m due to the outlier scale stated in Section 3), the effective quantization levels 2^8 * m_i / m results in only 2-3 effective quantization levels, confirming the paper's claim in Section 3 that non-outlier channels are severely under-represented under per-tensor quantization.

The reproduction demonstrates the connection between low effective quantization levels and large quantization errors: for non-outlier channels with only 2-3 effective quantization levels, the quantization step size Delta = max(|X|)/127 is so large relative to the non-outlier channel magnitudes that rounding errors become dominant, confirming why per-tensor quantization of LLM activations leads to the accuracy degradation shown in Table 1 (e.g., OPT-175B dropping from 71.6% FP16 to 32.3% with INT8 per-tensor).

The implementation demonstrates that when per-tensor quantization is applied to an activation tensor containing outlier channels, the quantization step size Delta = max(|X|)/127 is dominated by the outlier channel magnitudes, making Delta very large relative to the magnitudes of non-outlier channels. Code computes and logs the ratio Delta / m_i for non-outlier channels, showing that this ratio is ~100x (consistent with the ~100x outlier scale stated in Section 3), which means that non-outlier channel values are effectively rounded to only 2-3 distinct integer levels, making rounding errors dominant for those channels.

The reproduction verifies that for non-outlier channels with only 2-3 effective quantization levels (as derived from the formula 2^8 * m_i / m with m_i ~100x smaller than m), the quantization rounding error for a non-outlier value v is on the order of Delta/2 = max(|X|)/254, which is large relative to v itself (since v << max(|X|)). This is demonstrated by computing the expected absolute quantization error for non-outlier channels and showing it is comparable to or exceeds the actual non-outlier channel magnitudes, confirming why rounding errors become dominant for non-outlier channels under per-tensor quantization as described in Section 3.

The reproduction connects the theoretical 2-3 effective quantization levels analysis to the empirically observed accuracy degradation in Table 1: OPT-175B drops from 71.6% (FP16) to 32.3% (INT8 per-tensor), and similarly catastrophic drops are observed for OPT-6.7B (64.9% to 39.9%), OPT-13B (65.6% to 33.0%), OPT-30B (67.9% to 32.8%), and OPT-66B (69.5% to 33.1%). A result analysis note confirms that all per-tensor INT8 results approach near-random performance (~33% for 4-way classification), consistent with the paper's explanation that large quantization errors from 2-3 effective levels destroy model performance.

The simulated per-channel activation quantization experiment has been set up: OPT models of sizes 6.7B, 13B, 30B, 66B, and 175B are evaluated under four INT8 activation quantization schemes (per-tensor, per-token, per-channel, and FP16 baseline), measuring average accuracy on WinoGrande, HellaSwag, PIQA, and LAMBADA, as reported in Table 1.

Table 1 FP16 baseline results have been reproduced: OPT-6.7B achieves 64.9%, OPT-13B achieves 65.6%, OPT-30B achieves 67.9%, OPT-66B achieves 69.5%, and OPT-175B achieves 71.6% average accuracy on WinoGrande, HellaSwag, PIQA, and LAMBADA.

Table 1 INT8 per-tensor quantization results have been reproduced: OPT-6.7B achieves 39.9%, OPT-13B achieves 33.0%, OPT-30B achieves 32.8%, OPT-66B achieves 33.1%, and OPT-175B achieves 32.3% average accuracy, showing severe degradation compared to FP16.

Table 1 INT8 per-token quantization results have been reproduced: OPT-6.7B achieves 42.5%, OPT-13B achieves 33.0%, OPT-30B achieves 33.1%, OPT-66B achieves 32.9%, and OPT-175B achieves 31.7% average accuracy, confirming that per-token quantization only slightly improves over per-tensor and fails to address activation quantization difficulty.

Table 1 INT8 per-channel quantization results have been reproduced: OPT-6.7B achieves 64.8%, OPT-13B achieves 65.6%, OPT-30B achieves 68.0%, OPT-66B achieves 69.4%, and OPT-175B achieves 71.4% average accuracy, confirming that simulated per-channel activation quantization successfully bridges accuracy with the FP16 baseline (within 0.2% across all model sizes).

The reproduction correctly notes that per-channel activation quantization (the only scheme that preserves accuracy in Table 1) is marked as incompatible with INT8 GEMM kernels (shown in gray in Table 1), because hardware-accelerated GEMM kernels only allow scaling along the outer dimensions (token dimension T for activations, output channel dimension C_o for weights) as expressed by Equation 2, not along the inner channel dimension C_i of activations.

The SmoothQuant smoothing transformation has been implemented, including the per-channel smoothing factor with migration strength alpha, offline fusion into previous layers, and application to transformer blocks with the Figure 6 precision mapping.

The mathematically equivalent per-channel smoothing transformation (Equation 3) has been implemented: Y = (X * diag(s)^{-1}) * (diag(s) * W) = X_hat * W_hat, where s is a per-channel smoothing factor vector of dimension C_i. The smoothed activation X_hat = X * diag(s)^{-1} divides each input channel j of X by s_j, and the smoothed weight W_hat = diag(s) * W multiplies each input channel j of W by s_j, preserving the mathematical equivalence of the linear layer output Y.

The migration strength hyper-parameter alpha and the per-channel smoothing factor formula (Equation 4) have been implemented: s_j = max(|X_j|)^alpha / max(|W_j|)^(1-alpha) for j = 1, 2, ..., C_i, where max(|X_j|) is the maximum absolute value of the j-th input channel of the activation (estimated from calibration samples) and max(|W_j|) is the maximum absolute value of the j-th input channel of the weight matrix. The implementation supports arbitrary alpha values in [0, 1] and uses alpha=0.5 as the default for OPT and BLOOM models and alpha=0.75 for GLM-130B.

The per-channel activation statistics max(|X_j|) for computing smoothing factors are estimated offline using 512 random calibration sentences from the Pile pre-training dataset, as described in Section 4 and Section 5.1. The calibration is performed once and the resulting smoothing factors s_j are fixed and reused for all downstream tasks without any re-calibration.

The smoothing factor s has been implemented to be fused offline into the parameters of the preceding layer (e.g., the previous linear layer or layer norm) rather than applied as a separate runtime scaling kernel. Specifically, when the input X is produced from a previous linear operation (linear layer or layer norm), the division by diag(s) is absorbed into the previous layer's parameters offline, adding no extra kernel call overhead at inference time. For the case where input comes from a residual add, an extra scaling is added to the residual branch as described in Section 4.

Code has been implemented to visualize the effect of SmoothQuant smoothing with alpha=0.5 on activation and weight distributions, reproducing Figure 5. The visualization must show: (left) the original activation with large outlier channels and the corresponding flat weight distribution; (middle/right) after applying the smoothing transformation with s_j = max(|X_j|)^0.5 / max(|W_j|)^0.5, both the smoothed activation X_hat and the scaled weight W_hat have more balanced per-channel magnitudes, making both easier to quantize.

The precision mapping for transformer blocks described in Section 4 and shown in Figure 6 has been implemented. Specifically: (1) inputs and weights of compute-heavy operators—linear layers (self-attention Q/K/V projections, output projection, feed-forward layers) and BMM operators in attention—are quantized to INT8; (2) lightweight element-wise operations including ReLU, Softmax, and LayerNorm keep activations in FP16; (3) the smoothing transformation is applied to the input activations of self-attention and feed-forward layers before quantization. All matrix multiplications (GEMMs and BMMs) in the transformer block use W8A8 INT8 kernels.

The INT8 W8A8 quantization has been applied to all linear layers in the transformer block, including the self-attention Q, K, V projection linear layers, the self-attention output projection linear layer, and all feed-forward network (FFN) linear layers. Both the weights and input activations of each of these linear layers are quantized to INT8 before the GEMM operation, using CUTLASS INT8 GEMM kernels as described in Section 4 and Section 5.1.

The INT8 W8A8 quantization has been applied to the self-attention Q (query) projection linear layer. Both the weight matrix W_Q and the input activation X fed into this linear layer are quantized to INT8 before the GEMM operation using CUTLASS INT8 GEMM kernels, as described in Section 4 and Section 5.1. The SmoothQuant smoothing transformation is applied to the input activation of this layer before quantization.

The INT8 W8A8 quantization has been applied to the self-attention K (key) projection linear layer. Both the weight matrix W_K and the input activation X fed into this linear layer are quantized to INT8 before the GEMM operation using CUTLASS INT8 GEMM kernels, as described in Section 4 and Section 5.1. The SmoothQuant smoothing transformation is applied to the input activation of this layer before quantization.

The INT8 W8A8 quantization has been applied to the self-attention V (value) projection linear layer. Both the weight matrix W_V and the input activation X fed into this linear layer are quantized to INT8 before the GEMM operation using CUTLASS INT8 GEMM kernels, as described in Section 4 and Section 5.1. The SmoothQuant smoothing transformation is applied to the input activation of this layer before quantization.

The INT8 W8A8 quantization has been applied to the self-attention output projection linear layer (the linear layer that projects the concatenated attention head outputs back to the model dimension). Both the weight matrix W_O and the input activation (the post-attention, pre-residual tensor) are quantized to INT8 before the GEMM operation using CUTLASS INT8 GEMM kernels, as described in Section 4 and shown in Figure 6.

The INT8 W8A8 quantization has been applied to all feed-forward network (FFN) linear layers in the transformer block (e.g., the up-projection and down-projection linear layers, or the two linear layers in a two-layer FFN). Both the weight matrix and the input activation of each FFN linear layer are quantized to INT8 before the GEMM operation using CUTLASS INT8 GEMM kernels. The SmoothQuant smoothing transformation is applied to the input activations of the FFN block before quantization into the first FFN linear layer, as described in Section 4 ('we perform scale smoothing for the input activations of self-attention and feed-forward layers') and shown in Figure 6.

The original FP16 linear modules in both the PyTorch Huggingface and FasterTransformer backends have been replaced with INT8 linear modules backed by CUTLASS INT8 GEMM kernels. Specifically, the implementation replaces the floating point linear modules and bmm function with INT8 kernels as stated in Section 5.1: 'we implement INT8 linear modules and the batched matrix multiplication (BMM) function with CUTLASS INT8 GEMM kernels. We simply replace the original floating point (FP16) linear modules and the bmm function with our INT8 kernels as the INT8 model.'

The INT8 W8A8 quantization has been applied to the batched matrix multiplication (BMM) operators in the attention computation, specifically the QK^T score computation and the score-V weighted sum, as stated in Section 4 ('We also quantize BMM operators in the attention computation') and shown in Figure 6. Both operands of each BMM are quantized to INT8 and the BMM is executed using an INT8 GEMM kernel.

The lightweight element-wise operations in the transformer block—specifically Softmax, LayerNorm, and ReLU—keep their activations in FP16 and are NOT quantized to INT8, as described in Section 4 ('keeping the activation as FP16 for other lightweight element-wise operations like ReLU, Softmax, and LayerNorm') and shown in Figure 6. These operations produce FP16 outputs that may be subsequently re-quantized to INT8 at the next compute-heavy operator boundary.

The SmoothQuant per-channel smoothing transformation (Equation 3, s_j = max(|X_j|)^alpha / max(|W_j|)^(1-alpha)) is applied at the input activations of the self-attention block and the feed-forward (FFN) block before INT8 quantization, as stated in Section 4 ('we perform scale smoothing for the input activations of self-attention and feed-forward layers'). The smoothing factor is fused offline into the preceding layer parameters (e.g., LayerNorm weights or previous linear layer weights) so no extra runtime kernel is required, and the resulting smoothed activation X_hat is quantized to INT8 for the subsequent linear layers.

The full transformer block quantization flow shown in Figure 6 has been verified end-to-end: (1) FP16 residual additions and LayerNorm outputs feed into the SmoothQuant smoothing transformation; (2) smoothed activations are quantized to INT8 at the Q/K/V linear layer inputs; (3) INT8 BMMs compute attention scores and weighted values; (4) Softmax operates in FP16 on the INT8 BMM outputs after dequantization; (5) the output projection linear layer receives INT8 inputs; (6) the FFN receives smoothed INT8 inputs for its linear layers, with ReLU/activation functions in FP16 between FFN layers. The implementation matches the data format transitions shown in Figure 6.

The reproduction verifies that the migration strength alpha correctly controls the balance of quantization difficulty between activations and weights: when alpha is too small (pushing difficulty to activations), activation quantization error is large; when alpha is too large (pushing difficulty to weights), weight quantization error is large; only in the sweet spot (alpha=0.4-0.6 for OPT/BLOOM, alpha=0.75 for GLM-130B) are both activations and weights easy to quantize. This is consistent with the ablation in Figure 10 and the discussion in Section 4.
The experimental setup has been reproduced, including baselines, O1-O3 quantization settings, models, datasets, calibration on Pile, and the PyTorch/FasterTransformer INT8 kernel implementations.

The four baseline INT8 post-training quantization methods have been implemented for comparison: (1) W8A8 naive quantization with per-tensor dynamic activation quantization and per-tensor weight quantization; (2) ZeroQuant with group-wise weight quantization and per-token dynamic activation quantization; (3) LLM.int8() with per-channel weight quantization and per-token dynamic activation quantization plus FP16 for outliers; and (4) Outlier Suppression with per-tensor static activation quantization and per-tensor weight quantization, as specified in Table 2.

The three SmoothQuant efficiency levels O1, O2, and O3 have been implemented as defined in Table 2: SmoothQuant-O1 uses per-tensor weight quantization and per-token dynamic activation quantization; SmoothQuant-O2 uses per-tensor weight quantization and per-tensor dynamic activation quantization; SmoothQuant-O3 uses per-tensor weight quantization and per-tensor static activation quantization. All three settings use INT8 for both weights and activations unless specified otherwise.

The three families of LLMs used for accuracy evaluation have been acquired and configured: OPT models (Zhang et al., 2022) at sizes 6.7B, 13B, 30B, 66B, and 175B; BLOOM-176B (Scao et al., 2022); and GLM-130B (Zeng et al., 2022). The migration strength alpha=0.5 is used for all OPT and BLOOM models, and alpha=0.75 for GLM-130B (whose activations are more difficult to quantize with ~30% outliers), as stated in Section 5.1.

The OPT model family (Zhang et al., 2022) has been acquired and configured at all five sizes used in the accuracy experiments: OPT-6.7B, OPT-13B, OPT-30B, OPT-66B, and OPT-175B. The models are loaded via the PyTorch Huggingface backend (for OPT-6.7B, OPT-13B, OPT-30B single-GPU experiments) and the FasterTransformer backend (for OPT-13B, OPT-30B, OPT-66B, OPT-175B multi-GPU experiments). The migration strength alpha=0.5 is used for all OPT models as stated in Section 5.1.

The BLOOM-176B model (Scao et al., 2022) has been acquired and configured for SmoothQuant W8A8 quantization evaluation. The model is evaluated using the average accuracy on WinoGrande, HellaSwag, PIQA, and LAMBADA as reported in Table 4. The migration strength alpha=0.5 is used for BLOOM-176B as stated in Section 5.1, because its activation outliers are less severe than GLM-130B (the naive W8A8 baseline only degrades accuracy by 4% for BLOOM-176B).

The GLM-130B model (Zeng et al., 2022) has been acquired and configured for SmoothQuant W8A8 quantization evaluation using GLM-130B's official evaluation repository. The migration strength alpha=0.75 is used for GLM-130B because its activations are more difficult to quantize with ~30% outliers (as stated in Section 5.1 and Section 4). The top 2% tokens are clipped when calibrating static quantization step sizes for GLM-130B following Wei et al. (2022). Evaluation uses MMLU, MNLI, QNLI, and LAMBADA (not the standard OPT/BLOOM benchmarks, because some appear in GLM-130B's training set), as described in Section 5.1.

The evaluation datasets have been configured: seven zero-shot benchmarks—LAMBADA, HellaSwag, PIQA, WinoGrande, OpenBookQA, RTE, and COPA—plus the WikiText language modeling dataset are used to evaluate OPT and BLOOM models via lm-eval-harness. For GLM-130B, the evaluation uses MMLU, MNLI, QNLI, and LAMBADA via GLM-130B's official evaluation repository (because some of the other benchmarks appear in GLM-130B's training set), as described in Section 5.1.

The LAMBADA dataset (Paperno et al., 2016) has been configured as a zero-shot evaluation benchmark for OPT and BLOOM models via lm-eval-harness. LAMBADA measures language modeling accuracy on the final word of a passage and is also used as one of the four evaluation benchmarks for GLM-130B.

The HellaSwag dataset (Zellers et al., 2019) has been configured as a zero-shot evaluation benchmark for OPT and BLOOM models via lm-eval-harness, as described in Section 5.1. HellaSwag is used as one of the seven zero-shot benchmarks for OPT/BLOOM evaluation (e.g., reported individually in Table 3 for OPT-175B).

The PIQA dataset (Bisk et al., 2020) has been configured as a zero-shot evaluation benchmark for OPT and BLOOM models via lm-eval-harness, as described in Section 5.1. PIQA is one of the seven zero-shot benchmarks reported individually in Table 3 for OPT-175B and averaged in Table 4.

The WinoGrande dataset (Sakaguchi et al., 2019) has been configured as a zero-shot evaluation benchmark for OPT and BLOOM models via lm-eval-harness, as described in Section 5.1. WinoGrande is one of the seven zero-shot benchmarks reported individually in Table 3 for OPT-175B and is one of the four benchmarks averaged in Table 4 for OPT-175B and BLOOM-176B.

The OpenBookQA (Mihaylov et al., 2018), RTE (Wang et al., 2018), and COPA (Roemmele et al., 2011) datasets have been configured as zero-shot evaluation benchmarks for OPT and BLOOM models via lm-eval-harness, as described in Section 5.1. These three benchmarks are reported individually in Table 3 for OPT-175B as part of the 7-benchmark zero-shot evaluation suite.

The OpenBookQA dataset (Mihaylov et al., 2018) has been configured as a zero-shot evaluation benchmark for OPT and BLOOM models via lm-eval-harness, as described in Section 5.1. OpenBookQA is reported individually in Table 3 for OPT-175B (FP16: 34.0%, SmoothQuant-O1: 33.4%, SmoothQuant-O2: 33.0%, SmoothQuant-O3: 33.4%) and is one of the 7-benchmark zero-shot evaluation suite used to compute the average accuracy column in Table 3.

The RTE dataset (Wang et al., 2018) has been configured as a zero-shot evaluation benchmark for OPT and BLOOM models via lm-eval-harness, as described in Section 5.1. RTE is reported individually in Table 3 for OPT-175B (FP16: 59.9%, SmoothQuant-O1: 58.1%, SmoothQuant-O2: 59.6%, SmoothQuant-O3: 59.9%) and is one of the 7-benchmark zero-shot evaluation suite used to compute the average accuracy column in Table 3.

The COPA dataset (Roemmele et al., 2011) has been configured as a zero-shot evaluation benchmark for OPT and BLOOM models via lm-eval-harness, as described in Section 5.1. COPA is reported individually in Table 3 for OPT-175B (FP16: 88.0%, SmoothQuant-O1: 89.0%, SmoothQuant-O2: 88.0%, SmoothQuant-O3: 90.0%) and is one of the 7-benchmark zero-shot evaluation suite used to compute the average accuracy column in Table 3.

Table 3 results for OPT-175B on OpenBookQA, RTE, and COPA have been reproduced for all methods. OpenBookQA results: FP16 34.0%, W8A8 14.0%, ZeroQuant 17.8%, LLM.int8() 34.2%, Outlier Suppression 16.6%, SmoothQuant-O1 33.4%, SmoothQuant-O2 33.0%, SmoothQuant-O3 33.4%. RTE results: FP16 59.9%, W8A8 49.5%, ZeroQuant 50.9%, LLM.int8() 60.3%, Outlier Suppression 53.4%, SmoothQuant-O1 58.1%, SmoothQuant-O2 59.6%, SmoothQuant-O3 59.9%. COPA results: FP16 88.0%, W8A8 56.0%, ZeroQuant 55.0%, LLM.int8() 87.0%, Outlier Suppression 55.0%, SmoothQuant-O1 89.0%, SmoothQuant-O2 88.0%, SmoothQuant-O3 90.0%.

The WikiText language modeling dataset (Merity et al., 2016) has been configured as a perplexity evaluation benchmark for OPT and BLOOM models, as described in Section 5.1. WikiText perplexity is reported in Table 3 for OPT-175B (e.g., FP16 achieves 10.99, SmoothQuant-O3 achieves 11.17) and is also used for LLaMA model evaluation in Table 6 (WikiText-2, sequence length 512) and Table 7 (WikiText-2, sequence length 2048).

The WikiText language modeling dataset (Merity et al., 2016) has been configured as a perplexity evaluation benchmark for OPT-175B, as used in Table 3. The evaluation computes perplexity (lower is better) on the WikiText dataset and is run via lm-eval-harness alongside the seven zero-shot benchmarks for OPT and BLOOM models, as described in Section 5.1.

Table 3 WikiText perplexity results for OPT-175B have been reproduced for all methods: FP16 achieves 10.99, W8A8 achieves 93080, ZeroQuant achieves 84648, LLM.int8() achieves 11.10, Outlier Suppression achieves 96151, SmoothQuant-O1 achieves 11.11, SmoothQuant-O2 achieves 11.14, and SmoothQuant-O3 achieves 11.17. The results confirm that SmoothQuant-O3 maintains near-FP16 perplexity while baselines (W8A8, ZeroQuant, Outlier Suppression) catastrophically degrade to perplexity values in the tens of thousands.

The WikiText-2 dataset has been configured as a perplexity evaluation benchmark for LLaMA models (Touvron et al., 2023a) with a sequence length of 512, as used in Table 6. The evaluation measures perplexity (lower is better) on WikiText-2 for FP16 and W8A8 SmoothQuant variants of LLaMA-7B, LLaMA-13B, LLaMA-30B, and LLaMA-65B. The activation smoothing calibration procedure has been implemented: smoothing factors s_j (Equation 4) and static quantization step sizes are calibrated once using 512 random sentences sampled from the Pile pre-training dataset validation set. The migration strength alpha is selected via a quick grid search on a subset of the Pile validation set. For GLM-130B, the top 2% tokens are clipped when calibrating static quantization step sizes following Wei et al. (2022). The same calibrated smoothed and quantized model is then applied to all downstream evaluation tasks without any per-task re-calibration, as described in Section 5.1.

The PyTorch Huggingface backend for SmoothQuant has been implemented: INT8 linear modules and the batched matrix multiplication (BMM) function have been implemented with CUTLASS INT8 GEMM kernels. The original FP16 linear modules and bmm function are replaced with the INT8 kernels to create the INT8 model. This backend operates on a single GPU without model parallelism support (as noted in Section 5.3), and is used for proof-of-concept evaluation on OPT-6.7B, OPT-13B, and OPT-30B.

The FasterTransformer backend for SmoothQuant has been implemented: INT8 linear modules and BMM functions backed by CUTLASS INT8 GEMM kernels are integrated into the FasterTransformer serving framework. The implementation supports Tensor Parallelism (Shoeybi et al., 2019) for multi-GPU inference, enabling evaluation on OPT-13B, OPT-30B, OPT-66B, and OPT-175B in both single-GPU and multi-GPU configurations on NVIDIA A100 80GB GPU servers, as described in Section 5.3.

The range of memory reduction factors across all four sequence lengths in Table 10 has been verified to span approximately 1.92x to 1.97x: specifically 1040/527 ≈ 1.97x (seqlen=128), 1054/533 ≈ 1.98x (seqlen=256), 1068/545 ≈ 1.96x (seqlen=512), and 1095/570 ≈ 1.92x (seqlen=1024). All four values fall within a range consistent with the paper's claim of 'halving memory usage' for serving MT-NLG 530B as stated in Section 5.4.

It has been verified that all four SmoothQuant INT8 memory footprints in Table 10 (527GB at seqlen=128, 533GB at seqlen=256, 545GB at seqlen=512, 570GB at seqlen=1024) fit within the aggregate GPU memory capacity of a single 8-GPU node of A100 80GB GPUs (8×80GB = 640GB total), confirming that SmoothQuant enables serving MT-NLG 530B within a single node as stated in Section 5.4.

It has been verified that all four FP16 memory footprints in Table 10 (1040GB at seqlen=128, 1054GB at seqlen=256, 1068GB at seqlen=512, 1095GB at seqlen=1024) exceed the 640GB aggregate memory capacity of a single 8-GPU A100 80GB node, confirming that FP16 serving of MT-NLG 530B requires 16 GPUs (two nodes) and that SmoothQuant's INT8 quantization is necessary to enable single-node serving as claimed in Section 5.4.
The ablation studies on quantization scheme latency and migration strength alpha have been reproduced, matching Table 11 and Figure 10.

The quantization scheme latency benchmark has been executed using the PyTorch Huggingface implementation, measuring GPU inference latency (in ms) for FP16, LLM.int8(), SmoothQuant-O1, SmoothQuant-O2, and SmoothQuant-O3 on OPT-13B and OPT-30B at sequence lengths 256 and 512, with batch size 4, on NVIDIA A100 80GB GPUs, as reported in Table 11.

Table 11 GPU latency results for OPT-13B (batch size 4) have been reproduced: at sequence length 256, FP16 achieves 152.6ms, LLM.int8() achieves 237.1ms, SmoothQuant-O1 achieves 124.5ms, SmoothQuant-O2 achieves 120.5ms, SmoothQuant-O3 achieves 112.1ms; at sequence length 512, FP16 achieves 296.3ms, LLM.int8() achieves 371.5ms, SmoothQuant-O1 achieves 243.3ms, SmoothQuant-O2 achieves 235.1ms, SmoothQuant-O3 achieves 223.1ms.

Table 11 GPU latency results for OPT-30B (batch size 4) have been reproduced: at sequence length 256, FP16 achieves 343.0ms, LLM.int8() achieves 387.9ms, SmoothQuant-O1 achieves 246.7ms, SmoothQuant-O2 achieves 240.2ms, SmoothQuant-O3 achieves 227.6ms; at sequence length 512, FP16 achieves 659.9ms, LLM.int8() achieves 654.9ms, SmoothQuant-O1 achieves 490.7ms, SmoothQuant-O2 achieves 478.3ms, SmoothQuant-O3 achieves 458.4ms.

Table 11 results confirm the latency ordering described in Section 5.5: the coarser the quantization granularity (from O1 to O3, from dynamic to static), the lower the latency. Specifically, SmoothQuant-O3 < SmoothQuant-O2 < SmoothQuant-O1 < FP16 for both OPT-13B and OPT-30B at both sequence lengths. Additionally, LLM.int8() is mostly slower than the FP16 baseline (LLM.int8() > FP16 for OPT-13B at both sequence lengths and for OPT-30B at sequence length 256), while all three SmoothQuant variants achieve lower latency than FP16 under all settings.

Table 11 results confirm that the latency ordering SmoothQuant-O3 < SmoothQuant-O2 < SmoothQuant-O1 holds for both OPT-13B and OPT-30B at both sequence lengths 256 and 512. For OPT-13B at seqlen=256: O3=112.1ms < O2=120.5ms < O1=124.5ms. For OPT-13B at seqlen=512: O3=223.1ms < O2=235.1ms < O1=243.3ms. For OPT-30B at seqlen=256: O3=227.6ms < O2=240.2ms < O1=246.7ms. For OPT-30B at seqlen=512: O3=458.4ms < O2=478.3ms < O1=490.7ms. This confirms the Section 5.5 claim that coarser quantization granularity (from O1 to O3, dynamic to static) results in lower latency.

Table 11 results confirm that all three SmoothQuant variants (O1, O2, O3) achieve lower latency than the FP16 baseline under all four model-seqlen combinations, as stated in Section 5.5 ('SmoothQuant achieves lower latency compared to FP16 under all settings'). Specifically: OPT-13B seqlen=256 FP16=152.6ms vs O1=124.5ms, O2=120.5ms, O3=112.1ms; OPT-13B seqlen=512 FP16=296.3ms vs O1=243.3ms, O2=235.1ms, O3=223.1ms; OPT-30B seqlen=256 FP16=343.0ms vs O1=246.7ms, O2=240.2ms, O3=227.6ms; OPT-30B seqlen=512 FP16=659.9ms vs O1=490.7ms, O2=478.3ms, O3=458.4ms.

Table 11 results confirm that LLM.int8() is mostly slower than the FP16 baseline as stated in Section 5.5. Specifically, LLM.int8() is slower than FP16 for OPT-13B at both sequence lengths (237.1ms vs 152.6ms at seqlen=256; 371.5ms vs 296.3ms at seqlen=512) and for OPT-30B at sequence length 256 (387.9ms vs 343.0ms). The only case where LLM.int8() is comparable to FP16 is OPT-30B at seqlen=512 (654.9ms vs 659.9ms), which is still not faster. This confirms the paper's explanation that the mixed-precision activation representation in LLM.int8() introduces large latency overhead.

The migration strength alpha ablation experiment has been executed on OPT-175B with LAMBADA accuracy as the evaluation metric, running SmoothQuant W8A8 quantization for a range of alpha values from 0 to 1 (including at minimum alpha values of 0.0, 0.2, 0.4, 0.5, 0.6, 0.8, and 1.0) using the PyTorch or FasterTransformer backend, as described in Section 5.5 and shown in Figure 10.

A plot reproducing Figure 10 has been generated, showing LAMBADA accuracy on OPT-175B as a function of migration strength alpha. The x-axis shows alpha values from 0 to 1 and the y-axis shows LAMBADA accuracy. The plot must clearly show the accuracy curve with the sweet spot region and indicate the FP16 baseline accuracy for reference, consistent with Figure 10.

Figure 10 results have been reproduced, confirming the migration strength sweet spot described in Section 5.5: when alpha < 0.4, activations are hard to quantize and LAMBADA accuracy on OPT-175B is low (near-random); when alpha > 0.6, weights are hard to quantize and accuracy degrades again; only in the sweet spot region alpha in [0.4, 0.6] can both weights and activations be quantized with small errors, maintaining near-FP16 LAMBADA accuracy (~74-75%) on OPT-175B.

The Figure 10 ablation result confirms that alpha=0.5 is a well-balanced default for OPT and BLOOM models: at alpha=0.5, the LAMBADA accuracy on OPT-175B matches or nearly matches FP16 accuracy (~74.7%), consistent with the evenly-split quantization difficulty formula s_j = max(|X_j|)^0.5 / max(|W_j|)^0.5 described in Section 4 and the alpha=0.5 default used for all OPT/BLOOM models in Section 5.1.
